system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

Conversation started: Sunday, April 12, 2026 04:08 PM
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
Provider: openrouter

You are on a text messaging communication platform, Telegram. Please do not use markdown as it does not render. You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

messages.content:
Law Firm Wiki compiler — Batch 3 of 3. You are compiling PI practice knowledge from case activity logs into wiki articles.

1. Read /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md for the full compilation schema
2. Read ALL existing articles in /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/ and /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/connections/
3. For each case in /tmp/batch_3_cases.txt (66 cases), read the activity logs in cases/<slug>/Activity Log/. For cases with many logs (>50 files), sample the first 10 and last 10 chronologically.
4. UPDATE existing articles: increment evidence_count, upgrade confidence (5+ cases = medium, 10+ = high), add new evidence bullets
5. CREATE new articles only for genuinely new patterns not covered by existing articles
6. ANONYMIZE all PII — use 'Case A', 'Case B', or descriptive patterns
7. Write articles to /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/ and /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/connections/
8. Append a compilation summary to /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/log.md
9. Do NOT rewrite wiki/index.md — the parent will rebuild it after all batches

Focus on extracting: settlement patterns, negotiation tactics, treatment timelines, SOL management, adjuster behavior, lien resolution, demand strategies.

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md]
# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/CLAUDE.md]
# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

<!--
MAINTAINER NOTES (stripped before injection; free context)

This file is intentionally short. If it starts approaching 200 lines, split
topic-specific rules into `.claude/rules/*.md` and reference them here.

Do not duplicate DATA_CONTRACT.md content — @-import it. The contract is
authoritative; this file is orientation.

MEMORY.md is a regular repo file we @-import here. It is NOT the same as
Claude Code's auto-memory (~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/MEMORY.md) —
that's machine-local and managed by Claude Code itself. Our repo-level
MEMORY.md is the operational state tracker, committed to git.
-->

Good - I can see the case counts. Now let me build a script to extract and read the activity logs efficiently. I'll process cases in batches, sampling large ones (>50 logs) as specified:

function.name: terminal
function.arguments:
{"command": "cd /opt/data/FirmVault && mkdir -p /opt/hermes/extracted

# For each case, extract relevant logs to a combined file. Sample first 10 + last 10 for cases >50 files
while IFS= read -r slug; do
  dir="cases/$slug/Activity Log"
  if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then continue; fi
  
  files=$(ls -1 "$dir"/*.md 2>/dev/null | sort)
  count=$(echo "$files" | wc -l)
  
  if [ "$count" -gt 50 ]; then
    # Sample first 10 and last 10
    selected=$(echo "$files" | head -10; echo "$files" | tail -10)
    selected=$(echo "$selected" | sort -u)
  else
    selected="$files"
  fi
  
  outfile="/opt/hermes/extracted/${slug}.txt"
  echo "=== CASE: $slug (${count} logs, sampled) ===" > "$outfile"
  for f in $selected; do
    echo "--- $(basename "$f") ---" >> "$outfile"
    cat "$f" >> "$outfile"
    echo "" >> "$outfile"
  done
done < /tmp/batch_3_cases.txt

echo "Done extracting. File counts:"
ls -la /opt/hermes/extracted/ | wc -l", "timeout": 120}

{"command": "cd /opt/data/FirmVault && rm -rf /opt/hermes/extracted && mkdir -p /opt/hermes/extracted

while IFS= read -r slug; do
  dir="cases/$slug/Activity Log"
  if [ ! -d "$dir" ]; then continue; fi
  
  files=$(find "$dir" -name "*.md" -type f | sort)
  count=$(echo "$files" | wc -l)
  
  if [ "$count" -gt 50 ]; then
    # Sample first 10 and last 10
    selected=$(echo "$files" | head -10)
    selected="$selected
$(echo "$files" | tail -10)"
    selected=$(echo "$selected" | sort -u)
  else
    selected="$files"
  fi
  
  outfile="/opt/hermes/extracted/${slug}.txt"
  echo "=== CASE: $slug (${count} logs) ===" > "$outfile"
  echo "$selected" | while IFS= read -r f; do
    if [ -f "$f" ]; then
      echo "--- $(basename "$f") ---" >> "$outfile"
      cat "$f" >> "$outfile"
      echo "" >> "$outfile"
    fi
  done
done < /tmp/batch_3_cases.txt

echo "Done extracting."
ls /opt/hermes/extracted/ | wc -l
wc -c /opt/hermes/extracted/*.txt | tail -1", "timeout": 120}

betty-short-mva-5-30-2022.txt:I wanted to give you a heads up on this one. We called Ohio Medicaid again to see what was taking them so long to review our proposal for a reduction. You'll recall that they shifted gears on us a while back and forced us to resubmit everything we had already communicated to them to their portal (apparently emails were no longer sufficient). During our most recent conversation, they mentioned seeking a copy of the release from the liability carrier. Apparently, this is needed so they can corroborate the limited settlement amount. We also got the impression that they might be independently trying to obtain this document by making outreach to the liability carrier on this case. When we asked them to clarify their intentions, they declined to elaborate. I wanted to make you aware of this in case they try to reach out to you as well. Also, if a copy of the release is something that you would be willing to provide, please send it our way and we can forward it to Medicaid. It sounds like this is the new road block they're putting up and it's unlikely they'll continue the negotiations until they receive it.

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Negotiation After Demand
    19|
    20|> After a demand is sent, the case enters negotiation with the insurance adjuster. This phase involves waiting for and evaluating the initial offer, communicating with the client about the offer, potential counter-offers, and either settlement or escalation to litigation. The negotiation phase can be complicated by client dissatisfaction, offer rejection, and lien resolution.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Post-Demand Sequence
    25|1. **Demand Sent**: Follow-up task created for adjuster response
    26|2. **Adjuster Response**: Initial offer received (or adjuster requests more time/information)
    27|3. **Attorney Evaluation**: Attorney evaluates the offer against case value, medical specials, liens
    28|4. **Client Communication**: Client informed of offer and attorney recommendation
    29|5. **Decision Point**: Accept, counter-offer, or reject and prepare for litigation
    30|
    31|### Client Rejection Pattern
    32|A significant pattern emerges when clients reject settlement offers:
    33|- The case may be labeled "Settlement" prematurely (before client accepts)
    34|- Client calls in asking for an update, is told the case is "on settlement"
    35|- Client explicitly rejects the offer ("I am not settling")
    36|- The case must be re-classified from "Settlement" back to "Negotiation"
    37|- This back-and-forth creates frustration for all parties
    38|
    39|### Adjuster Follow-Up
    40|- Adjusters may need to be prompted for a response after demand
    41|- The firm sends follow-up communications to the adjuster
    42|- Lien amounts must be resolved during this phase to calculate the client's net recovery
    43|
    44|### Communication Challenges
    45|- Clients in negotiation become increasingly frustrated with the pace
    46|- Missed calls and voicemails escalate in urgency and frustration
    47|- Clients may threaten to "take another route" (change attorneys)
    48|- Regular, proactive communication is critical during this phase
    49|
    50|## Evidence
    51|
    52|- In an auto accident case, the demand was sent and the case moved to "Settlement" phase. However, the client explicitly refused the offer — a voicemail stated "I told you I am not settling for what you are... so come back." The phase had to be changed from "Settlement" back to "Negotiation." Staff confusion was evident: one team member noted "appears was settled but client didn't accept so maybe ongoing negotiations."
    53|- In the same case, the client's frustration escalated over months: text messages included "Hey are you going to get back with me or do I need to take another route. Let me know today" (6/2024) and multiple missed calls with voicemails demanding updates (3/2025, 8/2025, 10/2025, 2/2026, 3/2026). The attorney requested the adjuster pay a lien from Rawlings (10/2025), indicating lien resolution was happening during negotiation.
    54|- The case demonstrated a pattern where the firm told the client the case was "on settlement phase" and "we can't disclose the amount yet" — which created client frustration when the settlement did not materialize quickly.
    55|- In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2024, KY, negotiation phase), one of two BI carriers (a state mutual) settled quickly after demand, while the second carrier (a regional insurer) remained in active negotiation. The adjuster from the second carrier returned a voicemail indicating willingness to discuss the claim. This demonstrates a split-settlement pattern: when multiple BI carriers are involved, individual carriers may settle at different times, creating a phased resolution. The SOL countdown (180 days noted in an automated alert) remained active on the open claim, adding urgency to the second negotiation track.
    56|- In an auto accident case (DOI 4/2023, KY), the case bypassed the demand phase and moved directly from treatment to litigation due to disputed liability. During litigation, defense counsel offered to "negotiate 50/50." The attorney discussed this with the client, explaining the case issues and defense counsel's proposal. This confirms that in disputed-liability cases, negotiation may occur within the litigation context rather than following the standard demand-offer-counter sequence.
    57|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), the negotiation phase had a unique twist: the BI carrier tendered policy limits (25/50) within one day of the demand being sent, but settlement could not proceed because the limits had to be split among four injured plaintiffs represented by different firms. The negotiation shifted from firm-vs-insurer to inter-counsel allocation. The attorney emailed all co-plaintiffs' counsel to negotiate distribution. One attorney was slow to provide medical bills, stalling the process for months. The firm updated the client regularly but could only say "we are still waiting." When inter-counsel agreement was not reached, the case moved to litigation. This shows a novel negotiation pattern: the insurer has already paid limits, but the money cannot be distributed without all parties' agreement.
    58|- In Case R (auto accident, minor injury — airbag rash, DOI 4/2023), a $5,000 demand was sent to the BI carrier. The carrier responded with a $500 "nuisance offer." The firm refused to negotiate further until the defendant had been deposed. After the defendant's deposition (3/2025 — nearly 2 years after the accident), the negotiation was expected to resume. This demonstrates a pattern where the firm deliberately pauses negotiation to build evidentiary leverage through litigation discovery, rather than accepting lowball offers on minor injury cases.
    59|- In Case S (auto accident, government transit defendant, DOI 9/2021), a BI settlement offer of $15,000 was received from a government risk management entity during active litigation. The firm was simultaneously corresponding with the government entity's attorney about the case. The negotiation during litigation pattern — where offers continue even after complaint filing — is confirmed by this case. Additionally, multiple liens (Medicare, Humana at $1,513, and health insurance) had to be resolved in parallel with the negotiation, complicating the settlement math on a government-defendant case.
    60|- In 4 cases from this batch (DOIs mid-2025 to late-2025, KY), demand was sent via fax/email to BI adjusters with attached records/bills, and the paralegal followed up requesting offer status. In one case, the firm demanded "policy limits" and attached a narrative report as supplemental support. The adjuster responded but the offer details were not yet visible. In another, both sides denied liability (rental vehicle driver), with the paralegal reporting to the attorney "both sides are denying liability on this. Not sure how to proceed" — demonstrating that disputed liability can stall negotiation entirely. In a third case, the firm sent demand to the BI adjuster and then followed up 2 weeks later asking to "confirm received" — the follow-up cadence of 2 weeks post-demand is a consistent pattern.
    61|- In a negotiation-phase case (DOI mid-2025, KY), the client was discovered to be incarcerated (in county jail) during active negotiation.
    62|- In 4 additional small MVA cases (KY, DOIs 6/2025-9/2025, negotiation phase), post-demand patterns confirmed: (1) In one case, the BI adjuster reviewed the PIP log after receiving the demand and found discrepancies: "what PIP has paid is significantly higher than the records from the demand show" — the adjuster requested additional records before making an offer. The firm provided the records and the adjuster responded with "increased offers for your clients" (companion case with coordinated offers). This confirms that adjusters use PIP payment data as a cross-check against demand records, and discrepancies delay offers. (2) In a multi-carrier case (2 BI carriers + 1 PIP), the firm sent demands to each BI carrier independently and followed up 3 days later requesting receipt confirmation. One carrier (Progressive) had not responded after 2 follow-ups over 2 weeks. (3) In one case, the demand was sent to National General BI via fax and email. No response was documented in the activity logs over 5+ months post-demand — confirming the "black hole" pattern where some carriers simply do not acknowledge demands. (4) In a companion case pair, the demand follow-up was "Demand Sent - Follow up with BI Adjuster" — a standardized Filevine task auto-generated upon demand dispatch.
    63|
    64|## Agent Guidance
    65|
    66|1. After demand is sent, do NOT prematurely label the case as "Settlement" until the client has agreed to accept an offer
    67|2. When an offer is received, schedule a call with the client to discuss — do not communicate offers via text
    68|3. If the client rejects an offer, document the rejection clearly and move the case back to "Negotiation"
    69|4. Maintain regular contact during negotiation (at minimum every 2 weeks)
    70|5. When the client asks for updates, provide specific information about what is happening and what's next
    71|6. During negotiation, resolve liens in parallel — don't wait until after settlement to address them
    72|7. If the client threatens to leave, escalate to the attorney immediately for a personal call
    73|8. Track every client contact attempt (successful or missed) in the activity log
    74|
    75|## Pitfalls
    76|
    77|- Premature phase change to "Settlement" before client agreement
    78|- Telling the client the case is in "settlement" when it's actually in negotiation (creates false expectations)
    79|- Not communicating offer details to the client promptly
    80|- Allowing weeks/months of silence during active negotiation
    81|- Staff not knowing the current case status when client calls in
    82|- Not resolving liens during negotiation (delays final settlement)
    83|- Client losing trust due to poor communication (may terminate representation)
    84|
    85|## Related
    86|
    87|- [[demand-preparation-workflow]] — the demand that precedes negotiation
    88|- [[client-expectations-management]] — managing client expectations during the long negotiation phase
    89|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — all offers require attorney evaluation
    90|- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — SOL may force a decision during negotiation
    91|", "total_lines": 90, "file_size": 10400, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

     2|title: PIP Claims Management

     4|phase: [phase_1_file_setup, phase_2_treatment, phase_4_negotiation]
     5|category: financial
     6|tags: [pip, insurance, bills, medical-payments, ledger, kentucky]
     7|confidence: high
     8|evidence_count: 31
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - medical-bill-routing-workflow
    13|  - pip-ledger-requests
    14|  - treatment-gap-defense
    15|  - va-lien-management
    16|  - multi-carrier-negotiation
    17|---
    18|
    19|# PIP Claims Management
    20|
    21|> Personal Injury Protection (PIP) is a first-party insurance benefit (from the client's own policy or the at-fault party's policy in KY) that covers medical bills regardless of fault. Managing PIP claims is a critical parallel workflow that runs alongside the BI claim. PIP must be actively directed to pay bills, and its ledger must be tracked for demand preparation.
    22|
    23|## Pattern
    24|
    25|PIP management follows this lifecycle:
    26|
    27|1. **Setup**: Send Letter of Representation to PIP carrier with claim number during file setup
    28|2. **Bill Routing**: As medical bills come in, forward them to PIP with a "direction to pay" (explicit instruction to process)
    29|3. **Ledger Tracking**: Periodically request PIP ledger updates to track what has been paid
    30|4. **Demand Integration**: The PIP ledger is needed before sending the BI demand to understand total medical specials and what remains unpaid
    31|5. **Exhaust Monitoring**: In some cases, PIP benefits may exhaust before all bills are paid
    32|
    33|Key observations from case data:
    34|- PIP carriers may sit on submitted bills for extended periods (30+ days)
    35|- Bills sometimes need to be re-sent when PIP does not process them
    36|- PIP adjusters may respond that they "still have benefits reserved" but have only one bill on file
    37|- Health insurance deductibles interact with PIP: bills may first go through health insurance, with the remainder then submitted to PIP
    38|- Indiana MVAs do NOT have PIP coverage (jurisdiction matters)
    39|
    40|## Evidence
    41|
    42|- In Case C, bills were sent to State Farm PIP on 11/1/2024, but as of 12/31/2024 (60 days later), NO PAYMENTS had been made by PIP. The attorney directed staff to resend and call PIP to identify the issue.
    43|- In Case B, the PIP adjuster from Erie Insurance responded to a ledger request noting "you still have benefits reserved" and only had one bill on file, despite multiple provider bills existing. Additional bills needed to be forwarded with direction to process.
    44|- In Case B, the PIP adjuster explained that one hospital bill was "applied to deductible" by health insurance, which is why PIP issued payment only to the radiology provider.
    45|- In Case A, the case was an Indiana MVA, meaning no PIP was available — this is a jurisdiction-specific distinction.
    46|- In Case D, PIP was used to pay a VA lien directly. The firm sent a "direction to pay" the VA lien ($4,437.13) to the PIP carrier along with the VA billing statement. The PIP adjuster issued payment directly to the Department of Veterans Affairs. This demonstrates PIP's utility beyond paying medical providers — it can satisfy government liens.
    47|- In the same case, the PIP adjuster disputed the relatedness of chiropractic treatment, noting records indicated the treatment was "related to a condition she experienced years prior" and requesting "additional documentation indicating relatedness." PIP adjusters perform their own relatedness analysis and will deny bills they consider unrelated to the accident.
    48|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA), two separate PIP claims were set up: one with Farmers Insurance (client's own carrier) and one with American Transit Insurance (NYC cab company involved in a separate incident). The PIP application for Farmers was received and returned in early Dec 2022. The case illustrates managing PIP claims across multiple carriers when there are concurrent accidents, requiring careful tracking of which bills are attributable to which accident for PIP purposes.
    49|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM), PIP was managed through Allstate Insurance, the client's own carrier. The PIP adjuster was contacted separately from the UIM adjuster at the same carrier (different adjuster and claim number). The firm requested the ER bill copy from PIP and saved acknowledgment with the PIP log. This demonstrates the pattern where PIP and UIM are both through the same carrier but managed on separate tracks.
    50|- In Case H (auto accident, stolen vehicle), a Kentucky Assigned Claims (KAC) claim was set up through Nationwide PIP. The KAC process applies when the at-fault driver has no insurance (because coverage was denied due to the stolen vehicle). The PIP adjuster from Nationwide (Beth Freeman) managed the KAC claim, which followed a different process than standard PIP — requiring the firm to submit specific documentation and the adjuster to coordinate with other parties regarding billing disputes. The attorney agreed during an EUO that certain bills would not be submitted to KAC for payment, showing that the KAC process involves negotiation about which bills are covered.
    51|- In 13 cases from this batch (all KY MVAs, DOIs 4/2025 to 12/2025), PIP claims were set up with major carriers including State Farm, Progressive, Root Auto, Kentucky Farm Bureau, National General, Elco Insurance, Direct Auto Insurance, and SafeCo. Key patterns confirmed: (1) PIP LOR is sent on day one via fax/email to the PIP carrier; (2) PIP bills are submitted as they are received from providers with the standard request "please process the attached bill"; (3) PIP ledger requests are a recurring task — "please forward an updated PIP ledger" or "please forward a copy of the most recent PIP ledger" was observed in 8+ cases; (4) Same-carrier PIP and BI (e.g., Progressive handling both PIP and BI on separate claim numbers) requires careful claim number tracking; (5) In one case, the paralegal asked the PIP carrier to "process any remaining bills from [provider]" indicating batch bill submission at treatment completion.
    52|- PIP exhaustion was flagged as a concern in one case: "PIP may be exhausted. Sent request for ledger" — confirming that PIP ledger monitoring is critical to understand remaining benefits before demand preparation.
    53|- In a companion case involving 4 family members (2 adults, 2 minor children), the PIP adjuster completed the coverage investigation and communicated detailed payment decisions in a single email covering all four claimants: $4,000 reserved per person, specific billing codes denied per claimant (e.g., 97010 Hot/Cold Packs, 97014 E-stim, 97110 Therapeutic Exercise for some; 97140 Manual Therapy and 97110 for others), and ER bills excluded "per your direction." This is the most detailed PIP adjuster communication observed — showing that PIP adjusters perform individual line-item billing analysis and that the firm can direct PIP to exclude certain bill categories (such as ER bills).
    54|- In the same companion case, the PIP adjuster issued Reservation of Rights letters for the minor children because "proof of loss" could not be confirmed through the police report and claimant statement alone. Recorded interviews with the adult passengers were required as alternate proof of loss. This demonstrates that PIP coverage for passengers (especially minors) may require additional verification beyond the police report.
    55|- In a case with no PIP coverage available ("NO TREATMENT TO DATE AND NO PIP"), the BI claim proceeded independently without any PIP track. This confirms the jurisdiction-conditional nature of PIP: even in Kentucky, individual cases may lack PIP coverage if the client's own policy does not include it.
    56|- In a file-setup case, the firm faxed the PIP claim number to the carrier requesting acknowledgment within the first day of file setup, consistent with the standard LOR workflow. The carrier (Nationwide) was contacted via email to "forward the claim information or a copy of the denial of coverage" — confirming that claim status verification is done early.
    57|- In a case with an uninsured at-fault driver and no BI available, the attorney directed staff to check for household insurance before setting up KAC PIP — confirming the coverage investigation hierarchy: BI first, then UM/UIM, then household insurance, then KAC as last resort.
    58|- In 8 additional small MVA cases (KY, DOIs 6/2025-2/2026), PIP management was observed across State Farm (4 cases), Progressive (1), Liberty Mutual (1), National General (1), and Root Auto Insurance (1). Key observations: (1) PIP exhaustion triggers provider billing inquiries — in one case, a chiropractic/urgent care provider emailed requesting a PIP log and asking "please advise if more PIP is available or no funds till settlement"; the firm responded with a PIP exhaust letter; (2) In one case, PIP was confirmed as "plenty of PIP remaining" and the firm instructed the provider to resubmit billing directly to PIP; (3) State Farm PIP was managed through a centralized PIP team (separate from the BI adjuster) accessible via a dedicated phone extension; (4) One case with Root Auto Insurance as PIP carrier and a different carrier for BI confirmed the pattern of split-carrier PIP/BI management; (5) In one case, the PIP carrier (Progressive) requested recorded statements as part of a "proof of loss" investigation for PIP claims, scheduling it through the firm — this is distinct from the BI recorded statement and was handled as a separate event.
    59|- In one case (DOI 2/2026), the firm noted "NO PIP COVERAGE" at intake on the same day. The case proceeded with BI only (Travelers Insurance). This confirms that not all KY auto accident cases have PIP, and the coverage determination must be made immediately at intake. This is the second observed no-PIP case in the wiki.
    60|- Across 17 additional small cases (all KY, DOIs 5/2025-2/2026), PIP management patterns confirmed across 11 carriers: State Farm (4 cases), Kentucky Farm Bureau (3 cases), Progressive (2 cases), National General (1), Erie Insurance (1), Travelers (1), Shelter Insurance (1), Elco Insurance (1). Key confirmations: (1) PIP LOR and ledger requests sent on day one of file setup; (2) standard request language: "please forward an updated PIP ledger along with copies of any ER related records/bills received to date"; (3) PIP ledger requests sent to carrier email/fax within the first 2 weeks; (4) in one case, the paralegal requested PIP carrier "remove any reservation for our client and process any remaining billing that is NOT ER related treatment" — confirming that PIP carriers hold reservations on benefits and the firm must direct which bills to process; (5) in a companion case pair, both companions shared the same PIP carrier and claim number (Kentucky Farm Bureau #8069631) with the same adjuster handling both; (6) Erie Insurance PIP was set up for a case with UM through Esurance — confirming that PIP and UM can be through related but distinct carrier entities; (7) one case with PIP-only (no BI identified) confirmed the coverage investigation pattern where PIP is the only available benefit.
    61|
    62|## Agent Guidance
    63|
    64|1. Check jurisdiction first — Indiana MVAs do not have PIP; Kentucky MVAs do
    65|2. During file setup, send LOR to PIP carrier and request acknowledgment
    66|3. As each medical bill is received, forward to PIP with explicit "please process the attached and forward an updated PIP ledger upon completion"
    67|4. Set a 30-day follow-up task for each bill submitted to PIP
    68|5. If no PIP payment is confirmed within 30 days, resend the bill AND call the PIP adjuster
    69|6. Request PIP ledger updates before demand preparation
    70|7. Track which bills have been paid by PIP vs. health insurance vs. unpaid
    71|8. When health insurance pays part of a bill, understand the deductible interaction before submitting to PIP
    72|
    73|## Pitfalls
    74|
    75|- Assuming PIP will automatically process bills — they require explicit direction to pay
    76|- Sending bills once and not following up for months
    77|- Not tracking which bills PIP has vs. doesn't have on file
    78|- Forgetting that out-of-state accidents may not have PIP coverage
    79|- Not understanding the health insurance deductible / PIP interaction
    80|
    81|## Related
    82|
    83|- [[medical-bill-routing-workflow]] — the overall process for routing medical bills
    84|- [[pip-ledger-requests]] — specifically requesting and tracking PIP ledgers
    85|- [[treatment-gap-defense]] — unpaid bills and PIP delays can affect case timeline
    86|", "total_lines": 85, "file_size": 12343, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Demand Preparation Workflow
     3|slug: demand-preparation-workflow
     4|phase: [phase_3_demand]
     5|category: process
     6|tags: [demand, demand-letter, preparation, records, treatment-complete, phase-change]
     7|confidence: high
     8|evidence_count: 24
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - medical-records-collection
    13|  - case-phase-transitions
    14|  - attorney-review-triggers
    15|  - negotiation-after-demand
    16|---
    17|
    18|# Demand Preparation Workflow
    19|
    20|> The demand phase begins when the client's treatment is complete and all medical records/bills have been collected. The transition to "Demand in Progress" is a formal phase change that triggers a series of tasks: final records requests, records review for completeness and relatedness, lien identification, and ultimately the drafting and sending of the demand package to the insurance adjuster.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Prerequisites for Demand Phase Entry
    25|1. Client confirms treatment is complete (via phone call, documented in log)
    26|2. All known medical providers have been identified
    27|3. Outstanding records/bills requests have been sent
    28|4. Attorney or case manager authorizes the phase change
    29|
    30|### Demand Preparation Sequence
    31|1. **Phase Change**: Status changed from "Treatment" to "Demand in Progress" with reason logged
    32|2. **Final Records Requests**: Any outstanding records/bills are requested or re-requested
    33|3. **Records Review**: Medical records are reviewed for:
    34|   - Relatedness to the accident (unrelated treatment must be separated)
    35|   - Completeness (all visits documented)
    36|   - Referrals to other providers discovered in records (new providers must be added)
    37|4. **Lien Identification**: Health insurance liens identified and lien amounts requested (e.g., from Rawlings/WellCare)
    38|5. **Demand Drafting**: Attorney prepares the demand package
    39|6. **Demand Sent**: Package sent to the BI adjuster; follow-up task created
    40|
    41|### Records Review Reveals New Providers
    42|A critical pattern: when records from one provider are received and reviewed, they may reference visits to other providers not previously on file. These must be added and their records requested before the demand can proceed.
    43|
    44|### Adjuster Proactive Contact During Demand Prep
    45|Insurance adjusters may proactively call to check on claim status during the demand preparation period, especially as the case approaches SOL. This should be documented and the adjuster given a general update without committing to specific timelines.
    46|
    47|## Evidence
    48|
    49|- In an auto accident case (DOI 2/2023), the client confirmed treatment was complete during a phone call on 3/11/2024 ("done treating with all medical providers listed on file. No more hospital visits."). Phase was changed to "Demand in Progress" that same day. Records review revealed a referral to White House Clinics and King's Eye Care not previously on file — both had to be added and records requested. Lien request was sent to The Rawlings Company (WellCare) during this phase. The BI adjuster (Clearcover) proactively called to follow up on claim status.
    50|- In the same case, records from providers were separated for relatedness (unrelated visits excluded) before demand preparation.
    51|- The demand was eventually sent, but the case then moved to "Settlement" and then back to "Negotiation" when the client refused the offer, indicating the demand-to-settlement pipeline is not always linear.
    52|- In Case M (MVA, DOI 8/2023), the demand preparation used EvenUP (a third-party AI demand tool) to generate the initial demand package. The attorney saved the EvenUP output to the file, then directed staff to edit it and send to both BI carriers. The demand was sent to both carriers on the same day (1/10/2024), with the case simultaneously transitioning from "Demand in Progress" to "Negotiation." The adjuster acknowledged receipt and promised evaluation "by the end of this week." The next day, policy limits (25/50) were tendered. This demonstrates the fastest observed demand-to-offer turnaround: 1 day from demand sent to limits tendered. EvenUP integration into demand preparation is a significant workflow accelerator.
    53|- In a treatment-phase MVA case (DOI 8/2024), the attorney directed: "Please see records and bills that we are missing. She's probably done treating at this point, and we can send the demand." Records were then requested from a dental school. The pattern of attorney-initiated demand readiness check followed by staff records gap-fill is consistent across cases.
    54|- In a treatment-phase MVA case (DOI 5/2024), the firm noted "spoke with client, he's sending over some bills he received. Need to review to make sure we have everything and move forward with demand." Follow-up requests for outstanding records/bills were sent. A PIP ledger update was requested. The SOL was 90 days away, creating urgency. The demand preparation was gated on receiving records from 3+ providers.
    55|- In a treatment-phase MVA case (DOI 5/2024), the attorney noted at intake: "Will need 5 years of priors from all providers other than EMS." This expanded the records-collection scope well beyond accident-related treatment, requiring historical medical records that take longer to obtain and increase demand preparation timeline.
    56|- In a trucking MVA case (DOI 2/2024), outstanding medical bills from a treatment provider were discussed in correspondence between the firm and the provider's billing office. The case was in litigation, but records completeness was still being addressed — demonstrating that demand preparation gaps can persist even after a case enters litigation.
    57|- In 6 cases from this batch (DOIs mid-to-late 2025, KY), the standard demand preparation workflow was observed: treatment confirmed complete, records/bills requested from all providers, paralegal followed up with providers for outstanding items, then demand sent to BI adjuster with records/bills attached. In one case, the firm sent a narrative report as supplemental support and demanded "policy limits." In another, a second round of records was needed from a provider who had done additional treatment after the initial demand materials were assembled. The pattern of "sent demands on both but [client] went back for some additional treatment with [provider] which she just completed. Sent request over to [provider] this morning. Should be quick to get wrapped up" confirms that late treatment can delay demand even after an initial package is prepared.
    58|- In cases with companion plaintiffs, the demand for both clients was sometimes sent to the same adjuster simultaneously — "I've attached the records/billing for [companion] as well. Please consider this our demand for policy limits." This batch-demand approach for companions sharing the same BI carrier is efficient but requires separate tracking.
    59|- The pattern of PIP ledger requests before demand finalization was confirmed across 6+ cases: firms routinely request PIP ledgers from carriers to understand what has been paid and what remains before calculating the demand amount. "Please forward a PIP ledger for our client" is a standard pre-demand task.
    60|- In cases with client at fault (1 case) and no BI/UM coverage (1 case), demand was never prepared — the case was declined before reaching that phase. This confirms that demand preparation requires viable BI or UM recovery path.
    61|- In an MVA case (DOI 7/2025), the demand workflow proceeded rapidly: treatment complete, records/bills collected from 4 providers, PIP ledger requested, and BI carrier tendered policy limits ($25K). The firm then transitioned to the UM claim track, sending a COOTS letter. The demand-to-settlement on the BI track was completed in approximately 8 months from DOI, which is among the faster timelines observed for a case with 4 providers.
    62|- In a demand-phase MVA case (DOI 8/2025), the firm sent demand materials directly to a third-party claims administrator (CCMSI) rather than a traditional insurance carrier. The demand requested policy limits tender. When the initial adjuster didn't respond, the demand was re-sent to a different adjuster at the same TPA. This confirms that TPA-managed BI claims follow the same demand workflow but may require adjuster escalation within the TPA structure.
    63|- In a slip-and-fall demand case (DOI 4/2025), the demand was gated on records from a specialist (orthopedic) that the firm was having difficulty obtaining despite multiple fax requests over months. The entry "having issues obtaining norton ortho records. resending requests" illustrates how a single provider's non-responsiveness can block the entire demand timeline.
    64|- In an MVA demand case (DOI 12/2025), the firm requested a PIP ledger and provided it to the treatment provider along with the message "Ledger attached. 7k paid to [provider]. Please resubmit billing." This confirms the PIP-ledger-to-provider coordination step before demand: providers need to know what PIP has already paid so they can adjust their billing, which then affects the demand amount calculation.
    65|- In 7 additional small MVA cases (KY, DOIs 6/2025-11/2025), the demand preparation workflow was confirmed with consistent patterns: (1) treatment completion triggers the "Demand in Progress" or "Demand Sent" phase change; (2) records/bills from all providers must be collected before demand can proceed; (3) in cases with PIP exhaustion, the PIP exhaust letter was sent to providers simultaneously with final records requests, combining two steps; (4) demand was faxed to the BI adjuster with "please acknowledge receipt" follow-up email; (5) in multi-carrier cases (2 observed), demands were sent to each BI carrier independently; (6) in a companion case pair, both demands were prepared simultaneously once both clients completed treatment, confirming that synchronized demand preparation is more efficient; (7) PIP ledger requests were sent to the PIP carrier as a pre-demand step in every case with PIP coverage, consistent with earlier observations.
    66|- In one case (DOI 8/2025, multi-carrier), the demand was sent to Progressive Insurance BI by fax, followed by an email follow-up 3 days later: "Please confirm receipt. If you're no longer the bodily injury adjuster assigned to this claim, please provide the updated adjuster's name and contact information." This follow-up template addresses the common problem of adjuster reassignment that can cause demands to go unacknowledged.
    67|- In one case (DOI 8/2025), the firm added a companion's records/billing to the same demand communication: "I've attached the records/billing for [companion] as well. Please consider this our demand for policy limits." This shows that in companion cases with the same BI carrier, the demand for multiple plaintiffs can be combined in a single communication.
    68|
    69|## Agent Guidance
    70|
    71|1. When a client reports treatment is complete, confirm via phone and document the call
    72|2. Trigger the phase change to "Demand in Progress" and log who authorized it
    73|3. Immediately create tasks for any outstanding records/bills requests
    74|4. When records arrive, review them for references to other providers — add any new providers found
    75|5. Separate records by relatedness to the accident
    76|6. Send final health insurance lien requests (to Rawlings, Optum, etc.) to get lien amounts
    77|7. Compile all records, bills, and lien information for the attorney's demand preparation
    78|8. When an adjuster calls proactively, document the contact and provide a general update
    79|9. After demand is sent, create a 30-day follow-up task for adjuster response
    80|
    81|## Pitfalls
    82|
    83|- Moving to demand before all providers are identified (records review may reveal new ones)
    84|- Not separating related vs. unrelated treatment in the medical records
    85|- Forgetting to request health insurance lien amounts before the demand
    86|- Not documenting the client's confirmation that treatment is complete
    87|- Failing to follow up with the adjuster after demand is sent
    88|- Assuming the demand-to-settlement path is linear (client may reject the offer)
    89|
    90|## Related
    91|
    92|- [[medical-records-collection]] — records must be complete before demand
    93|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — demand is a formal phase change with prerequisites
    94|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — attorney reviews and approves the demand
    95|- [[negotiation-after-demand]] — what happens after the demand is sent
    96|", "total_lines": 95, "file_size": 12252, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

     2|title: Companion Case Management
     3|slug: companion-case-management
     4|phase: [phase_0_onboarding, phase_1_file_setup, phase_7_litigation]
     5|category: process
     6|tags: [companion, family, multiple-plaintiffs, coordination, deposition]
     7|confidence: medium
     8|evidence_count: 10
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - intake-and-onboarding-workflow
    13|  - client-communication-through-family
    14|  - litigation-process-patterns
    15|---
    16|
    17|# Companion Case Management
    18|
    19|> When multiple family members are injured in the same accident, each client requires a separate case file but the cases must be coordinated. Companion cases share the same defendant, insurance carriers, and litigation timeline, but each plaintiff has independent medical treatment, damages, and settlement negotiations. The primary client (typically the parent/driver) often serves as the coordinator for all cases.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|1. **Intake**: One family member signs up first; firm discovers additional family members during the welcome call or initial contact
    24|2. **File Creation**: Each companion gets a separate case file with separate intake documents
    25|3. **Coordination**: Parent/primary client is expected to facilitate the companion's document signing, provide their information (SSN, medical providers, insurance)
    26|4. **Records Collection**: Each companion's medical providers must be identified and records requested independently — this often falls on the primary client to facilitate
    27|5. **Litigation**: Companions may be joined in one lawsuit (e.g., "Plaintiff A et al. vs. Defendant") or filed separately. Depositions are scheduled sequentially for companions
    28|6. **Settlement**: Each companion's settlement is evaluated independently, but mediation may address all cases simultaneously
    29|
    30|## Evidence
    31|", "total_lines": 74, "file_size": 13295, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=31 to continue reading (showing 1-30 of 74 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

     2|title: Underinsured Motorist (UIM) Claim Management
     3|slug: uim-claim-management
     4|phase: [phase_1_file_setup, phase_4_negotiation, phase_7_litigation]
     5|category: financial
     6|tags: [uim, underinsured-motorist, insurance, allstate, parallel-claim, consent-to-settle]
     7|confidence: medium
     8|evidence_count: 9
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - pip-claims-management
    13|  - multi-carrier-negotiation
    14|  - litigation-process-patterns
    15|  - statute-of-limitations-monitoring
    16|---
    17|
    18|# Underinsured Motorist (UIM) Claim Management
    19|
    20|> When the at-fault driver's BI policy limits are insufficient to cover the plaintiff's damages, the plaintiff may pursue an Underinsured Motorist (UIM) claim against their own auto insurance carrier. UIM claims run as a parallel track to the BI claim and require distinct procedural steps including consent-to-settle notifications, separate complaint filing, and independent adjuster management — often with the same carrier that provides the client's PIP coverage.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|UIM claim management follows a parallel-track structure alongside the BI claim:
    25|
    26|### Setup
    27|1. **Identify UIM Coverage**: During file setup, check whether the client's own auto policy includes UIM coverage. If the at-fault driver's BI limits are low relative to the client's injuries, UIM becomes relevant.
    28|2. **Separate Claim Setup**: The UIM claim requires its own claim number and adjuster, even when the UIM carrier is the same company providing PIP.
    29|3. **Consent to Settle**: Before accepting the BI settlement, the UIM carrier must be notified and given an opportunity to consent (or subrogate). This is a critical procedural requirement.
    30|
    31|", "total_lines": 93, "file_size": 11105, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=31 to continue reading (showing 1-30 of 93 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Kentucky Assigned Claims (KAC) Process
     3|slug: kentucky-assigned-claims-process

    16|
    17|# Kentucky Assigned Claims (KAC) Process
    18|
    19|> When the at-fault driver in a Kentucky MVA has no valid insurance coverage (due to coverage denial, policy lapse, or being uninsured), the plaintiff can file a claim through the Kentucky Assigned Claims Plan (KAC). KAC assigns the claim to a carrier (e.g., Nationwide) who acts as the PIP carrier for medical bill payments. The KAC process differs from standard PIP in its documentation requirements, bill submission rules, and the potential for disputes about which bills are covered.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|The KAC claim lifecycle:
    24|
    25|1. **Identification**: During file setup, when BI coverage is denied or the at-fault driver is uninsured, identify the need for a KAC claim
    26|2. **Filing**: Submit a KAC application with the required documentation (accident report, proof of no coverage, client information)
    27|3. **Assignment**: KAC assigns the claim to a carrier. In observed cases, Nationwide was the assigned carrier.
    28|4. **Adjuster Assignment**: The assigned carrier appoints an adjuster who manages the claim similarly to standard PIP but with additional requirements
    29|5. **Bill Submission**: Medical bills are submitted to the KAC carrier for payment, but the carrier may dispute which bills are covered
    30|6. **Lien Coordination**: KAC payments interact with health insurance liens (e.g., Medicaid/Conduent) — the firm must track what KAC pays versus what the health insurer paid

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Medicaid Lien Management (Conduent)
    19|
    20|> When a PI client is covered by Medicaid, the state asserts a subrogation lien for accident-related medical expenses paid by Medicaid. In Kentucky, Medicaid subrogation is administered by Conduent (a third-party administrator). Medicaid liens differ from private health insurance liens in that they involve government authority, can be substantial (covering hospital stays and emergency treatment), and the lien administrator actively monitors cases and follows up with law firms.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|The Medicaid lien lifecycle:
    25|
    26|1. **Early Identification**: At intake, determine whether the client has Medicaid coverage. If yes, send a first lien letter to the Medicaid subrogation administrator (Conduent in Kentucky) immediately.
    27|2. **Lien Assertion**: Conduent will respond with a lien amount based on Medicaid payments made for accident-related treatment. Response times are typically faster than VA but slower than private insurers.
    28|3. **Active Monitoring**: Unlike private health insurance lien holders who are largely passive, Conduent actively monitors cases — they will call the firm to check on case status.
    29|4. **Lien Coordination**: Medicaid lien amounts must be coordinated with other payment sources (PIP, KAC, BI settlement). Bills paid by Medicaid cannot also be claimed in the demand as unpaid specials.
    30|5. **Dual Lien Possibility**: A client may have both Medicaid (Anthem Medicaid) and private health insurance (Anthem BCBS) liens on the same case — these are separate entities requiring separate tracking.
    31|
    32|### Key Differences from Private Health Insurance Liens
    33|
    34|| Aspect | Private Health Insurance | Medicaid (Conduent) |
    35||--------|------------------------|---------------------|
    36|| Legal basis | State subrogation law / ERISA | Federal/state Medicaid law |
    37|| Administrator | Insurance company's subrogation dept | Conduent (third-party administrator) |
    38|| Monitoring | Generally passive | Active — calls firm to check status |
    39|| Lien amounts | Typically moderate | Can be very large (hospital stays covered) |
    40|| Negotiation | Percentage reductions common | Government program — less flexibility |
    41|| Coordination | With PIP/settlement | With PIP, KAC, and settlement |
    42|
    43|## Evidence
    44|
    45|- In Case F (auto accident, dental injury), both Anthem Blue Cross Blue Shield and Anthem Medicaid asserted liens of $425.41 on the same case. The firm tracked these as separate liens, using ZipLiens for the health insurance lien. The dual-lien scenario required the firm to identify which entity was the correct recipient for lien correspondence — an error in routing (sending to the wrong Anthem entity) caused delays. This demonstrates that "Anthem" can represent both private insurance AND Medicaid, and the firm must distinguish between them.
    46|- In Case H (auto accident, stolen vehicle/coverage denial), Conduent asserted a Medicaid lien of $20,669.08 — the largest single lien observed in the case. The firm sent a "First Lien Letter faxed to Conduent" at file setup. Conduent later called the firm to follow up on case status (voicemail from Conduent on 2/5/2025). The firm provided the Conduent lien information and medical records/bills to the KAC adjuster during the claims process. The substantial Medicaid lien (covering a multi-day hospital stay at the University of Louisville Hospital) illustrates that Medicaid lien amounts can be proportionally larger than private insurance liens because Medicaid pays a higher share of hospital costs for low-income clients.
    47|- In an MVA case (DOI 8/2024, KY), Conduent asserted a Medicaid lien of $3,830 (amount confirmed). The client had ongoing dental treatment at a university dental school. Conduent called the firm to check on case status — the note read: "Conduent called for status of her case — case is still open — client still having dental treatment." The adjuster from the BI carrier (Progressive) also called to get treatment status information. This case confirms the active-monitoring pattern: Conduent proactively calls for case status updates, unlike private insurers who typically wait passively. The firm must be prepared to provide status updates to both the BI carrier and the Medicaid lien holder concurrently.
    48|
    49|## Agent Guidance
    50|
    51|1. At intake, ask whether the client has Medicaid coverage. If yes, flag the case as "Medicaid lien likely"
    52|2. Send a first lien letter to Conduent immediately during file setup — do not delay
    53|3. Track the Medicaid lien SEPARATELY from any private health insurance lien, even if both are through "Anthem"
    54|4. When Conduent calls to check on case status, respond promptly — they are actively monitoring
    55|5. Before preparing the demand or settlement math, obtain the current Medicaid lien amount from Conduent
    56|6. Coordinate Medicaid lien payments with PIP and KAC payments — ensure no double-billing
    57|7. When presenting settlement options to the attorney, include the Medicaid lien as a mandatory deduction
    58|8. Use ZipLiens or similar tracking tools to monitor Medicaid liens alongside health insurance liens
    59|
    60|## Pitfalls
    61|
    62|- Confusing Anthem Medicaid with Anthem BCBS (they are separate entities with different lien processes)
    63|- Not sending the first lien letter to Conduent at file setup (delays lien identification)
    64|- Not responding to Conduent's follow-up calls (they may escalate or assert incorrect lien amounts)
    65|- Not factoring the Medicaid lien into settlement math early (Medicaid liens can be the largest single deduction)
    66|- Assuming Medicaid liens can be negotiated down as easily as private health insurance liens
    67|- Not tracking Medicaid lien payments alongside KAC/PIP payments
    68|
    69|## Related
    70|
    71|- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — contrast with private health insurance lien process
    72|- [[va-lien-management]] — another government lien with distinct handling
    73|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP payments may offset Medicaid lien obligations
    74|- [[kentucky-assigned-claims-process]] — KAC payments interact with Medicaid liens
    75|", "total_lines": 74, "file_size": 6467, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    65|2. `government-entity-records-collection` — How VA/government records collection differs from private providers; 12-18 month timelines; multiple fax lines; MyHealtheVet portal
    66|3. `adjuster-reassignment-patterns` — Multiple adjuster changes on single claim; "closer" adjuster pattern; fresh-eyes opportunity; communication impacts

    73|1. `lien-management-health-insurance` — Added VA cross-reference and government vs. private lien distinction

    80|
    81|### Key Patterns Identified
    82|- **VA liens are fundamentally different from private health insurance liens**: Federal legal authority, OGC Revenue Law Group as counterpart, GCL case numbers, months-long timelines
    83|- **VA records collection creates the worst bottleneck observed**: 12-18 months from request to receipt; records arrive incomplete; client must supplement
    84|- **PIP can pay VA liens directly**: A useful strategy to resolve the VA lien early using PIP benefits
    85|- **Adjuster reassignment can be an opportunity**: New "closer" adjusters are proactive and motivated to settle
    86|- **Unrelated treatment attribution is a variant of the treatment gap defense**: Adjuster does not deny treatment but attributes it to a different injury
    87|- **Client communication failures escalate dramatically in long cases**: 2+ year cases with VA delays produce the most extreme client frustration
    88|- **Circular dependency trap**: VA wants treatment dates to reopen case; firm needs records to identify dates — break with client MyHealtheVet access
    89|- **Attorney must eventually make the call to proceed with imperfect information**: Waiting for perfect VA records can extend cases indefinitely

   346|### Key New Patterns Identified
   347|- **UIM claims require a separate litigation track**: When BI limits are insufficient, UIM complaints are filed against the client's own insurer in a separate court, with separate discovery and defense counsel

   350|- **Medicaid liens (Conduent) are actively monitored**: Unlike passive private insurer liens, Conduent calls the firm to check on case status — and Medicaid liens can be the largest single deduction
   351|- **Dental injuries create unique timeline pressure**: Staged implant procedures (18+ months) push cases toward the SOL, often requiring litigation filing before treatment is complete
   352|- **Same carrier for multiple claim types creates confusion**: When PIP, BI, or UIM are at the same carrier, different adjusters handle each track — but the firm must carefully distinguish which claim it's communicating about
   353|- **Pre-settlement funding of $31K+ can exceed net recovery**: In complex cases with large liens and extensive treatment, pre-settlement funding can consume most or all of the client's share

   415|- **SOL pressure on transfer cases creates emergency filing**: When a case is transferred from prior counsel with SOL approaching (<60 days), the firm may need to onboard and file a complaint on the same day — skipping demand entirely

done", "timeout": 15}
